parts.title: Transcript
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
Speaker 1: You now have some time to look at the questions on your screen. Now answer the questions on your screen. Good morning and welcome to this environmental sciences lecture. Today, we will be discussing solar flares.

Speaker 1: It has been said that the cycles of solar flares affect the weather of the planet, the moods of its people, and even business cycles.

Speaker 1: When the sun was young, it was far more violent and active than it is today. It had a much faster rate of rotation. Its magnetic fields were stronger and less stable. And it produced even more powerful flares than those it produces today. But it was only 70% as bright as it is now.

Speaker 1: The sun became a mature star when it was about 5 to 10 solar years old, or 1 to 2 billion Earth years. Scientists believe that the sun will shine as it does today for billions of years. But in about 7 billion years, it will expand to 100 times its current size and become what is known as a red giant. Its colour will be deep red and its luminosity will be about 500 times greater than at present.

Speaker 1: After about 250 million years in the red giant phase, scientists expect that the sun will go through a helium flash, which means it will explode and eject about one-third of its mass into space. Thereafter, our sun's evolution is uncertain. It will probably go through a period of degeneration. When it is about 15 billion years old, or 75 solar years, it will probably become a white dwarf star with a diameter similar to the one of the earth's. It could finally become a black dwarf, which is essentially a small, cold, burnt-out shell.

Speaker 1: But we have a long way to go before the sun becomes a red giant or a white or black dwarf. Right now, the sun is in a long, quiet, relatively stable phase.

Speaker 1: So how do solar flares affect us on earth? Solar flares are huge gaseous outbursts from the sun. An average-sized flare may be more than 6,000 miles long. A flare can release the energy equivalent of 100 billion one megaton bombs.

Speaker 1: At the solar maximum, there may be as many as three to four small flares per hour and one enormous flare each month. But during the minimum, weeks or months may pass without a flare of any significance.

Speaker 1: Solar flares strengthen the intensity of the solar wind, which is a steady stream of ionized helium and hydrogen particles radiating outward from the sun at around 450 miles per second under great pressure, carrying about 1 million tons of gas per second.

Speaker 1: The solar wind, which reaches the earth in about four and a half days, impacts the magnetosphere, a magnetic field surrounding the earth, causing such phenomena as the aurora borealis and geomagnetic storms.

Speaker 1: The solar wind can generate radio waves on earth with a very long wavelength, known as extremely low frequency waves.

Speaker 1: The radiation generated by solar flares has a number of effects, the most clearly discerned of which is the disruption of radio communications.

Speaker 1: But some scientists also claim that everything from climatic changes to wars, earthquakes and flu epidemics are associated with increased solar activity. Other scientists dispute these claims and some spots and solar flares are certainly not the only cause of such disturbances.

Speaker 1: However, there is a correlation between solar activity and certain kinds of social, behavioural and geophysical effects.
